title: The Peaceful Pill Handbook
description: A manual that discusses assisted dying methods, end-of-life autonomy, and practical self-deliverance.
descriptionParagraphs: A manual that discusses assisted dying methods, end-of-life autonomy, and practical self-deliverance.

The Peaceful Pill Handbook is organized less as a story than as an argument. As a instructional manual, it tries to persuade readers through selection, emphasis, and direct claims about euthanasia, instruction, and public health.

Its significance lies in the way it compresses large claims into memorable formulas and positions. Even readers who reject the work usually have to reckon with how sharply it frames questions about euthanasia, instruction, and public health.

cover.alt: The Peaceful Pill Handbook cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A manual that discusses assisted dying methods, end-of-life autonomy, and practical self-deliverance.
banningParagraphs: The Peaceful Pill Handbook entered censorship debates as a instructional manual associated with euthanasia, instruction, and public health. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around self harm and instructional harm.

The earliest event currently captured here is 2007 onward in Australia, where Australian customs and classification authorities restricted circulation. Officials treated the book as an unacceptable guide to suicide and euthanasia methods. The case sits at the frontier between censorship, public health, and assisted-dying politics.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Suicide prevention
counterReadings.author: World Health Organization
counterReadings.note: Public-health counterpoint for instruction manuals centered on self-harm or assisted death.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: Included here as a civic counterpoint when bans blur personal autonomy, panic, and state control.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
